claim.statement_location: Abstract (final sentences) and Discussion (opening paragraphs) of the paper: the authors state that low executive function capacity and low Need for Cognition predicted larger increases in cognitive effort expenditure in response to monetary reward incentives, whereas individuals with greater executive function capacity and greater intrinsic motivation were less responsive to reward incentives.

data.source: Laboratory-collected behavioral data from a computerized experiment (Stroop task and a task-switching paradigm) with accompanying self-report questionnaires (Need for Cognition, BIS/BAS, Barratt Impulsiveness, GAD-7).
data.wave_or_subset: One Stroop block (120 trials: 30 incongruent, 90 congruent) followed by two task-switching blocks (within-subject reward manipulation: High Reward vs Low Reward; each block 140 trials).
data.sample_size: 54 recruited; 9 excluded (7 for <80% accuracy or missed deadlines, 2 for missing data), final N = 45 for primary analyses. NFC analyses excluded 2 additional participants (N = 43 for analyses involving NFC).
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual participant (subject-level), with trial-level mixed-effects models for RT and accuracy
data.access_details: Raw data available at the Open Science Framework: http://osf.io/gqvsh. No additional data access restrictions stated in the paper.
data.notes: Exclusion criteria: accuracy threshold (>=80%) and limits on missed response deadlines (excluded if missed >=15 deadlines in any block). RTs were log-transformed for analyses. Stroop RT cost computed as difference between standardized RTs for incongruent vs congruent trials (used as EF measure). Task-switching blocks counterbalanced for reward condition. Analyses used mixed-effects regression; trial/block practice effects were modeled (linear predictor of trial block).

method.description: Participants completed questionnaires (Need for Cognition, BIS/BAS, Barratt Impulsiveness, GAD-7), then a computerized Stroop task (one block of 120 trials) to assess executive function (Stroop RT cost). They then performed two counterbalanced task-switching blocks (140 trials each) in which monetary reward for correct responses was manipulated within-subject (High Reward = 5 cents vs Low Reward = 1 cent). Task-switching required responding to magnitude on red cards and parity on black cards; cue and stimulus were simultaneous. Performance-contingent cash bonuses were promised. Reaction times (RTs) on correct trials were log-transformed; mixed-effects regression models were used to examine effects of trial type (switch vs repeat), reward condition, Stroop RT cost (EF), and Need for Cognition, including interactions and block (practice) effects. Accuracy was analyzed with logistic mixed-effects models.
method.steps: 1) Recruit participants (McGill participant pool and university community), obtain consent. 2) Administer questionnaires: NFC (18-item), BIS/BAS, Barratt Impulsiveness, GAD-7. 3) Stroop task practice then one block of 120 Stroop trials (30 incongruent, 90 congruent); collect RTs and accuracy. 4) Compute Stroop RT cost per participant (standardized RT difference incongruent - congruent) to index EF. 5) Inform participants they will receive performance-contingent bonus. 6) Run first task-switching block (140 trials; reward condition indicated at trial start: 5c or 1c), with simultaneous cue-stimulus and 3s response window; provide feedback. 7) Run filler task and 8-min break. 8) Run second task-switching block with the alternate reward condition. 9) Apply exclusion criteria (accuracy <80% or >=15 missed deadlines in any block). 10) Analyze trial-level RTs (correct trials only; log-transformed) and accuracy using mixed-effects regressions (lme4 in R), including predictors: trial type, reward, Stroop RT cost (or NFC), block, and their interactions. 11) Report group-level and individual-differences effects (including extraction of per-subject regression coefficients where noted).
method.models: Linear mixed-effects regression models for RTs (log-transformed) using lme4 in R; logistic mixed-effects regression for accuracy. Models included fixed effects for trial type (switch vs repeat), reward condition (High vs Low), Stroop RT cost (continuous) or NFC (continuous), block (linear predictor), and relevant interactions (notably three-way interactions trial type × reward × Stroop RT cost and trial type × reward × NFC). Random effects structure conditioned on group-level estimates (per-subject coefficients visualized).
method.outcome_variable: Primary: task-switching reaction times (RTs), specifically switch costs (RT difference between task switch and task repeat trials). Secondary: response accuracy on Stroop and task-switching trials; Stroop RT cost used as predictor (index of executive function).
method.independent_variables: Reward condition (High vs Low), trial type (switch vs repeat), Stroop RT cost (individual EF measure), Need for Cognition (NFC) score, and interactions thereof.
method.control_variables: Block (linear predictor to account for practice effects), Behavioral Activation Scale (BAS) scores were included as covariates in supplementary analyses to control for individual reward sensitivity; trials with incorrect responses excluded from RT analyses.
method.tools_software: Analyses conducted in R using the lme4 package for mixed-effects regressions (Pinheiro & Bates reference cited). RTs log-transformed following Ratcliff (1993). Logistic regression used for accuracy models. No code repository link provided in paper.

results.summary: The Stroop task produced the expected incongruency effects (longer RTs and lower accuracy on incongruent trials). Typical task-switch costs were observed (slower and less accurate on switch vs repeat trials). There was no significant main effect of reward at the group level on switch costs. However, individual differences moderated the effect of reward: a significant three-way interaction showed that individuals with larger Stroop RT costs (lower EF) exhibited a reduction in switch costs on High Reward blocks (i.e., they increased cognitive effort in response to reward), whereas high-EF individuals did not show this reward-induced reduction. Similarly, Need for Cognition moderated reward effects: low-NFC individuals showed reward-induced switch cost reductions, whereas high-NFC individuals showed the opposite pattern. These interactions remained after controlling for BAS (reward sensitivity). Effects were present in RTs but not in accuracy.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Task switch effect (RT: switch vs repeat)
numerical_results.direction: positive (switch trials slower than repeat trials)
null (no significant group-level effect of reward)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Three-way interaction: Trial type × Reward × Stroop RT cost (RT model)
numerical_results.direction: negative (greater Stroop RT cost associated with larger reward-induced reduction in switch cost)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Three-way interaction: Trial type × Reward × NFC (RT model)
numerical_results.direction: positive (higher NFC associated with reward-induced increase in switch cost; lower NFC associated with reward-induced reduction in switch cost)
negative (switch trials slightly less accurate than repeat trials)

metadata.original_paper_title: Cognitive capacity limitations and Need for Cognition differentially predict reward-induced cognitive effort expenditure
